feynman.0: The electricity gets made a long way from where you plug things in. So on this page let’s build up the words we need for talking about an AC supply — rms, peak and peak-to-peak — then meet the transformer, which trades voltage for current, and finally look at the losses in the wires that make high-voltage transmission the only sensible way to push power across the country.
feynman.1: The coil in an alternator spins around at a steady rate, so the voltage it puts out goes up and down like a sine wave, swinging between +Vpeak and −Vpeak many times every second. Now if you just average that voltage over time, you get zero — every positive half-cycle gets exactly undone by the negative half-cycle right after it. And yet the thing still heats your toaster! Here’s the trick: heating doesn’t care which way the current runs, so the supply hands over power on both halves of the cycle. That tells us we need a number built out of power, not out of a plain average.
feynman.2: The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is just this: the steady DC voltage that would cook up the same average power in the same resistor. The RMS current means the same thing. Notice the definition says nothing whatever about sine waves — but for the sinusoid an alternator actually produces, the RMS values come out to be the peak values divided by √2:
feynman.3: The Australian mains is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. As far as power goes, every outlet acts just like a 230 V DC supply — but the real voltage is busy swinging between +325 V and −325 V (because 230 × √2 = 325 V), a peak-to-peak swing of about 650 V, and it runs through fifty whole cycles a second, one cycle every 1/50 = 0.020 s.
feynman.4: There are three numbers, and they all describe the very same sinusoid: Vpeak = √2 × VRMS, the peak-to-peak value Vp–p = 2Vpeak, and so Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. The RMS one is always the smallest of the three, and whenever somebody quotes you an AC voltage — on a power point or stamped on an appliance — it’s an RMS value, unless it tells you otherwise. And the same three descriptions, with the same conversions, work for the current too.
feynman.5: Because we built the RMS values out of power equivalence in the first place, the DC power relation just carries straight over to AC:
feynman.6: Key idea An RMS value is the steady DC voltage (or current) that would develop the same average power in the same resistor. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
feynman.7: Trap A globe glows exactly as bright on a 6.0 V battery as on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What’s the same is the average power — not the average voltage, which is zero for the AC supply, and not the maximum voltage, which for the AC supply is 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V. And reading the mains label “230 V” as if it were a peak value is the very same mistake, just turned around.
feynman.8: A transformer is nothing but two separate coils wound on a closed iron core — no moving parts, and no electrical connection between the coils at all. Alternating current in the primary coil turns it into an electromagnet whose magnetic flux swings along with the supply, and the iron core shepherds nearly all of that flux around the loop and through the secondary coil. So the flux threading every turn of the secondary is changing all the time, which means an EMF gets induced across it — electromagnetic induction with nothing spinning anywhere, as shown in Figure 8.1.
feynman.9: Every turn of the secondary wraps around the same changing flux, so each one chips in equally to the output: put in twice the secondary turns and you get twice the output voltage. For an ideal transformer — one that loses no power at all — the voltages come out in the same ratio as the turns:
feynman.10: A step-up transformer has more turns on the secondary than the primary (N2 > N1) and hands you a higher voltage than you fed it; a step-down transformer does the opposite. We name them for what they do to the voltage, and what counts is the ratio of the turns, not how many there actually are. The output alternates at the same frequency as the input — a transformer rescales the sinusoid, it doesn’t reshape it.
feynman.11: An ideal transformer passes every bit of its input power straight through to the output:
feynman.12: Trap A step-up transformer is not an amplifier — don’t let the name fool you. Push the voltage up by a factor of n and the current drops by exactly that same factor, because the output power can never beat the input power. That’s why the current ratio in the transformer equation sits upside-down compared to the other two: the high-voltage side of a transformer always carries the smaller current.
feynman.13: The whole business rests on flux that’s changing, and that’s exactly why transformers have to have AC. Hook the primary up to a battery instead, and the steady current makes a constant flux in the core; the flux through the secondary stops changing, so no EMF gets induced and the output is zero. Only right at the instant you connect or disconnect the battery — the only moments the flux actually moves — do you get a brief little pulse. So any grid that leans on transformers has got to be an AC grid.
feynman.14: Ishva’s phone charger steps the 230 V RMS Australian mains supply down to 11.5 V RMS. We’ll treat the transformer inside as ideal, with 600 primary turns, and the charger supplies 1.2 A RMS from its secondary coil.
feynman.16: Current drawn from the mains: I1 = I2 × V2V1 = 1.2 × 11.5 ÷ 230 = 0.060 A.
feynman.17: Let’s check it with power conservation: P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W and P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W — equal, just as an ideal transformer demands. We used RMS values the whole way through: the transformer ratios work just fine with RMS, peak or peak-to-peak values, as long as you stick to one kind on both sides.
feynman.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 is just energy conservation showing up yet again. The volt is a joule per coulomb — that’s W = qV from AOS 2 — so P = VI is counting the joules delivered every second, and an ideal transformer has to hand every one of those joules on.
feynman.19: A transmission system comes in three pieces wired in series: a supply, a pair of long lines, and a load — maybe a single machine, maybe a house, maybe a whole town. We’ll model the two lines as one resistor with total resistance Rline, as shown in Figure 8.2. With no transformers anywhere in it, the supply, the lines and the load make one series loop, so one single current Iline runs through the lot of them.
feynman.20: The lines follow Ohm’s law like any other resistor: shoving the current through them costs you a potential difference, and burns off power as heat.
feynman.21: Both formulas use the line current and the line resistance. The voltage in them is Vdrop, the drop along the lines themselves — never the supply voltage. The supply voltage splits up between the lines and the load, so the load only gets what’s left over: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.
feynman.22: Trap The most damaging slip in this whole topic is jamming the supply voltage in where Vdrop belongs — for instance, working out the line current as Vsupply ÷ Rline, or the loss as Vsupply² ÷ Rline. The full supply voltage is never sitting across the lines on their own; only Vdrop = IlineRline is. Work from the line current, or from the difference between the voltages at the two ends of the lines — never straight from the supply voltage.
feynman.23: Shanna’s community energy group runs a small wind turbine that generates 20 kW, fed to a dairy 2.0 km away through lines of total resistance 4.0 Ω. All the voltages and currents here are RMS values, and the turbine supplies its full 20 kW in both of the arrangements below.
feynman.24: Without transformers, the power leaves the turbine at 500 V. We get the line current from P = VI: Iline = 20 000 ÷ 500 = 40 A. The lines burn off Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — that’s 32% of everything generated. The drop along the lines is Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V, so the dairy is left with only 500 − 160 = 340 V and 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW.
feynman.25: With transformers, a step-up transformer at the turbine boosts the transmission voltage tenfold, up to 5.0 kV, and a step-down transformer at the dairy brings it back down to something usable. That same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The loss collapses to Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — just 0.32% — and the drop to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer gets 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and 19.9 kW makes it all the way to the dairy.
feynman.26: The numbers are showing us the general rule. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, so transmitting at n times the voltage cuts the current down by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss drops by a factor of n². Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A and the loss fell a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. And that’s the whole case for high-voltage transmission — the only other way, getting Rline down with thicker wire, means hanging far heavier and far pricier conductors on every single tower.
feynman.27: Stepping down again at the far end is just as necessary: it brings the voltage back to the safe, standard 230 V that appliances are built for — and it pushes the current back up, so the biggest currents anywhere in the whole system end up flowing in the local wiring, not out in the transmission lines.
feynman.28: Key idea For a fixed power delivery, stepping the transmission voltage up by a factor of n cuts the line current by a factor of n and the line loss by a factor of n². Losses depend on the line current and the line resistance — never work them out from the supply voltage.

sagan.0: Electricity is born far from the places where it is finally spent — in some distant valley, on some windswept ridge, then carried across the dark to the rooms where we live. This page builds the language we need to speak of an AC supply — rms, peak and peak-to-peak — then the transformer, that quiet device which trades voltage for current, and finally the losses in the lines that make high-voltage transmission the only way the world can afford to move its power.
sagan.1: The coil of an alternator turns at a steady, patient rate, and so its output voltage rises and falls in a smooth sinusoid, swinging between +Vpeak and −Vpeak many times in a single second. Take a plain average of that voltage over time and you find nothing at all — every positive half-cycle is undone by the negative half-cycle that follows it — and yet the supply still pours out heating power, faithfully and without pause. Heating, after all, does not care which way the current runs, so power is delivered on both half-cycles alike. To describe such a supply we must reach for a value founded on power itself, not on so empty a thing as a simple average.
sagan.2: The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is defined as the steady DC voltage that would raise the same average power in the same resistive component — a fair exchange, an alternating current measured against the calm DC that would do its equal. The RMS current is defined in just the same spirit. The definition says nothing of sine waves; but for the sinusoidal output of an alternator, the RMS values turn out to be the peak values divided by √2:
sagan.3: The Australian mains supply is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. For the purposes of power every outlet behaves as though it were a 230 V DC supply — yet the true voltage is never still, sweeping between +325 V and −325 V (for 230 × √2 = 325 V), a peak-to-peak swing of some 650 V, and running through fifty whole cycles every second, one complete cycle in 1/50 = 0.020 s.
sagan.4: Three voltages describe one and the same sinusoid: Vpeak = √2 × VRMS, the peak-to-peak value Vp–p = 2Vpeak, and so Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. Of the three the RMS value is always the smallest, and any AC voltage you meet — stamped on a power point or on an appliance plate — is an RMS value, unless it tells you otherwise. The very same three descriptions, with the very same conversions, hold for the current as well.
sagan.5: Because RMS values are built upon the equivalence of power, the DC power relation carries over to AC untouched, as though it had never left home:
sagan.6: Key idea An RMS value is the steady DC voltage (or current) that would raise the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
sagan.7: Trap A globe burns just as brightly on a 6.0 V battery as on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What the two share is the average power — not the average voltage, which for the AC supply is zero, and not the maximum voltage, which for the AC supply rises to 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V. To read the mains label “230 V” as a peak value is to commit the very same error, only turned around.
sagan.8: A transformer is two separate coils wound upon a single closed iron core — nothing moves within it, and the two coils never touch. Alternating current in the primary coil makes of it an electromagnet whose magnetic flux rises and falls with the supply, and the iron core gathers almost the whole of that flux and leads it faithfully around the loop and through the secondary coil. The flux threading every turn of the secondary is therefore forever changing, and so an EMF is induced across it — electromagnetic induction with not a single thing rotating, as shown in Figure 8.1.
sagan.9: Every turn of the secondary embraces the very same changing flux, so each one adds its equal share to the output: double the secondary turns and you double the output voltage. For an ideal transformer — one that lets slip no power at all — the voltages stand in the ratio of the turns:
sagan.10: A step-up transformer carries more secondary turns than primary turns (N2 > N1) and gives out a higher voltage than it is fed; a step-down transformer is its mirror image. Transformers take their names from what they do to the voltage, and it is the ratio of the turns, not the bare count of them, that decides everything. The output alternates at the same frequency as the input — a transformer rescales the sinusoid; it does not reshape it.
sagan.11: An ideal transformer hands the whole of its input power on to the output, keeping nothing back:
sagan.12: Trap A step-up transformer is no amplifier. Raise the voltage by a factor of n and you lower the current by that very same factor, for the output power can never rise above the input power — there is no free gift here. The current ratio in the transformer equation sits upside-down beside the other two: the high-voltage side of a transformer always carries the smaller current.
sagan.13: The whole mechanism rests upon changing flux, and herein lies the reason transformers must have AC. Connect the primary to a battery instead, and the steady current raises a constant flux in the core; the flux through the secondary changes no longer, so no EMF is induced and the output falls to nothing. Only in the fleeting instant the battery is joined or broken away — the only moments the flux stirs at all — does a brief pulse flicker into being. A grid that leans on transformers must therefore be an AC grid, through and through.
sagan.14: Ishva’s phone charger steps the 230 V RMS Australian mains supply down to 11.5 V RMS. The transformer within is taken to be ideal, with 600 primary turns, and the charger supplies 1.2 A RMS from its secondary coil.
sagan.16: Current drawn from the mains: I1 = I2 × V2V1 = 1.2 × 11.5 ÷ 230 = 0.060 A.
sagan.17: Check it against the conservation of power: P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W and P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W — equal, exactly as an ideal transformer demands. RMS values were used from start to finish: transformer ratios will work with RMS, peak or peak-to-peak values, so long as one kind alone is held to on both sides.
sagan.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 is the conservation of energy speaking once more. The volt is a joule for every coulomb — that is W = qV from AOS 2 — so P = VI is simply a tally of the joules delivered each second, and an ideal transformer must pass every last joule on.
sagan.19: A transmission system has three parts, strung together in series: a supply, a pair of long lines, and a load — whether a single machine, a single house, or a whole town asleep. The two lines are modelled as one resistor of total resistance Rline, as shown in Figure 8.2. sagan.20: The lines obey Ohm’s law as any other resistor must: to drive the current through them costs a potential difference, and squanders power as heat along the way.
sagan.21: Both formulas call upon the line current and the line resistance. The voltage that appears in them is Vdrop, the drop along the lines themselves — never the supply voltage. The supply voltage divides itself between the lines and the load, so the load receives only what remains: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.
sagan.22: Trap The most ruinous error in all this topic is to set the supply voltage where Vdrop belongs — reckoning, say, the line current as Vsupply ÷ Rline, or the loss as Vsupply² ÷ Rline. The full supply voltage is never across the lines alone; only Vdrop = IlineRline is. Work from the line current, or from the difference between the voltages at the two ends of the lines — never from the supply voltage directly.
sagan.23: Shanna’s community energy group runs a small wind turbine that generates 20 kW, fed to a dairy 2.0 km away through lines of total resistance 4.0 Ω. All voltages and currents are RMS values, and the turbine supplies its full 20 kW in both of the arrangements below.
sagan.24: Without transformers, the power leaves the turbine at 500 V. The line current follows from P = VI: Iline = 20 000 ÷ 500 = 40 A. The lines dissipate Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — fully 32% of the power generated, lost as warmth on the wind. The drop along the lines is Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V, so the dairy is left with only 500 − 160 = 340 V and 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW.
sagan.25: With transformers, a step-up transformer at the turbine lifts the transmission voltage tenfold, to 5.0 kV, and a step-down transformer at the dairy brings it back to a usable level. The same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The loss collapses to Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — a mere 0.32% — and the drop to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and 19.9 kW arrives, almost whole, at the dairy.
sagan.26: The numbers lay bare the general rule. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, so to transmit at n times the voltage is to cut the current by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss falls by a factor of n². sagan.27: Stepping down again at the journey’s end is no less needful: it returns the voltage to the safe, standard 230 V that our appliances are built to drink — and in doing so it raises the current once more, so that the largest currents in the whole vast system flow not in the distant transmission lines, but quietly in the wiring within our walls.
sagan.28: Key idea For a fixed power delivery, stepping the transmission voltage up by a factor of n cuts the line current by a factor of n and the line loss by a factor of n². Losses depend on the line current and the line resistance — never compute them from the supply voltage.

greene.0: Picture the journey hidden inside an ordinary wall socket. The RMS current is defined in precisely the same spirit. Notice that the definition says nothing at all about sine waves; it is more general than that. But for the smooth sinusoidal output of an alternator, the arithmetic settles into something beautifully simple — the RMS values turn out to be the peak values divided by √2:

But behind that calm number the real voltage is anything but steady: it surges between +325 V and −325 V (since 230 × √2 = 325 V), a peak-to-peak swing of about 650 V, and it does this fifty times every second, completing one full cycle every 1/50 = 0.020 s.

greene.6: Key idea An RMS value is the constant DC voltage (or current) that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
greene.7: Trap A globe shines just as brightly on a 6.0 V battery as on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply, and that is no accident. What the two share is the average power — not the average voltage, which is zero for the AC supply, and not the maximum voltage, which for the AC supply climbs to 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V. greene.8: A transformer is disarmingly simple to describe: two separate coils wound on a closed loop of iron, with no moving parts and no wire joining one coil to the other. The iron core then does the quiet, essential work — it gathers up almost all of that flux and guides it around the loop and straight through the secondary coil. greene.9: Every turn of the secondary embraces that same changing flux, so every turn pulls its weight equally in the output — double the secondary turns and you double the output voltage. For an ideal transformer, one that loses no power at all, the voltages stand in exactly the ratio of the turns:
greene.10: A step-up transformer carries more secondary turns than primary turns (N2 > N1) and hands back a higher voltage than it was given; a step-down transformer does the reverse. greene.11: An ideal transformer hands every watt of its input straight to the output:

That same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The current ratio in the transformer equation is upside-down relative to the other two. The high-voltage side of a transformer always carries the smaller current.

The flux through the secondary no longer changes, so no EMF is induced and the output is zero. Only at the instant the battery is connected or disconnected — the only moments the flux changes — does a brief pulse appear. A grid that lives on transformers must therefore be an AC grid.
hawking.14: Ishva’s phone charger steps the 230 V RMS Australian mains supply down to 11.5 V RMS. The transformer inside is modelled as ideal, with 600 primary turns, and the charger supplies 1.2 A RMS from its secondary coil.

hawking.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 is energy conservation, stated again. The volt is a joule per coulomb — that is W = qV from AOS 2 — so P = VI counts joules delivered each second, and an ideal transformer must pass every joule on.
hawking.19: A transmission system has three parts in series: a supply, a pair of long lines, and a load — a single machine, a house or a whole town. The two lines are modelled as one resistor of total resistance Rline, as shown in Figure 8.2. With no transformers in the system, the supply, the lines and the load form a single series loop, so one current Iline flows through all of them.

The supply voltage divides between the lines and the load, so the load gets only what is left: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.

The full supply voltage is never across the lines alone. Only Vdrop = IlineRline is. Work from the line current, or from the difference between the voltages at the two ends of the lines. Never from the supply voltage directly.
hawking.23: Shanna’s community energy group runs a small wind turbine that generates 20 kW, fed to a dairy 2.0 km away through lines of total resistance 4.0 Ω. All voltages and currents are RMS values, and the turbine supplies its full 20 kW in both arrangements below.

The lines dissipate Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — 32% of the power generated. The same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The loss collapses to Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — only 0.32% — and the drop to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and 19.9 kW reaches the dairy.

And since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss falls by a factor of n². Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A and the loss fell a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. This is the whole case for high-voltage transmission. The alternative — lowering Rline with thicker wire — means hanging far heavier and far more expensive conductors on every tower.
hawking.27: Stepping down at the destination matters just as much. It returns the voltage to the safe, standard 230 V that appliances are built for — and it raises the current again. So the largest currents in the whole system flow in the local wiring, not in the transmission lines.

nye.0: Here’s the thing about electricity — it gets made way out in the middle of nowhere, far from where you actually plug stuff in! So on this page we’re going to build the language for talking about an AC supply — rms, peak and peak-to-peak — then we’ll meet the transformer, this amazing gadget that swaps voltage for current, and finally we’ll tackle the line losses that make high-voltage transmission the one and only economical way to move power across the country.
nye.1: Check this out! The coil of an alternator spins at a nice steady rate, so its output voltage alternates like a sine wave, swinging between +Vpeak and −Vpeak over and over, many times every single second. Now if you just take a plain time-average of that voltage you get zero — every positive half-cycle gets cancelled out by the negative half-cycle right behind it! And yet an AC supply still delivers heating power. How? Because heating doesn’t care which way the current flows, so the supply hands you power on both half-cycles. That means describing an AC supply needs a value built on power — not on a simple average.
nye.2: So here’s the fix. The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is defined as the steady DC voltage that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. The RMS current is defined exactly the same way. Notice the definition says nothing at all about sine waves — but for the sinusoidal output of an alternator, the RMS values work out to be the peak values divided by √2:
nye.3: Take the Australian mains supply: it’s 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. For power purposes every outlet behaves just like a 230 V DC supply — but get this, the actual voltage swings between +325 V and −325 V (since 230 × √2 = 325 V) — that’s a peak-to-peak swing of about 650 V — and it rips through fifty cycles every second, one cycle every 1/50 = 0.020 s!
nye.4: Three voltages all describe the very same sinusoid: Vpeak = √2 × VRMS, the peak-to-peak value Vp–p = 2Vpeak, and putting those together, Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. The RMS value is always the smallest of the three — and here’s a handy rule: a stated AC voltage, whether it’s on a power point or stamped on an appliance plate, is an RMS value unless it tells you otherwise. The same three descriptions, with the same conversions, work for the current too.
nye.5: Now because RMS values are built right out of power equivalence, the DC power relation carries straight over to AC:
nye.6: Key idea An RMS value is the constant DC voltage (or current) that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
nye.7: Trap A globe shines just as bright on a 6.0 V battery as it does on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What’s the same is the average power — not the average voltage, which is zero for the AC supply, and not the maximum voltage, which is 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V for the AC supply. And reading the mains label “230 V” as a peak value? That’s the exact same mistake, just flipped around.
nye.8: Now feast your eyes on this gadget! A transformer is just two separate coils wound on a closed iron core — no moving parts, and no electrical connection between the coils at all. Alternating current in the primary coil turns it into an electromagnet whose magnetic flux alternates right along with the supply, and the iron core herds almost all of that flux around the loop and through the secondary coil. So the flux through every turn of the secondary is changing nonstop, which means an EMF gets induced across it — electromagnetic induction with nothing spinning, nothing rotating, as shown in Figure 8.1!
nye.9: Every single turn of the secondary wraps around that same changing flux, so each turn pitches in equally to the output: double the secondary turns and you double the output voltage! For an ideal transformer — one that loses zero power — the voltages come out in the ratio of the turns:
nye.10: A step-up transformer has more secondary turns than primary turns (N2 > N1) and hands you a higher voltage than you fed it; a step-down transformer does the opposite. Transformers get their names from what they do to the voltage, and here’s the key — it’s the ratio of the turns that matters, not how many turns there actually are. The output alternates at the very same frequency as the input — a transformer rescales the sinusoid; it doesn’t reshape it!
nye.11: An ideal transformer ships every bit of its input power straight out to the output:
nye.12: Trap Don’t get fooled — a step-up transformer is not an amplifier! Raise the voltage by a factor of n and you drop the current by that same factor, because the output power can never beat the input power. The current ratio in the transformer equation is flipped upside-down relative to the other two: the high-voltage side of a transformer always carries the smaller current.
nye.13: The whole show rests on changing flux — and that’s exactly why transformers demand AC! Hook the primary up to a battery instead, and the steady current makes a constant flux in the core; the flux through the secondary stops changing, so no EMF gets induced and the output is zero. The only time anything happens is the instant you connect or disconnect the battery — the only moments the flux actually changes — and even then you just get a quick little pulse. So a grid that runs on transformers has got to be an AC grid!
nye.14: Ishva’s phone charger takes the 230 V RMS Australian mains supply and steps it down to 11.5 V RMS. We’ll model the transformer inside as ideal, with 600 primary turns, and the charger supplies 1.2 A RMS out of its secondary coil. Let’s work it out!
nye.16: Current pulled from the mains: I1 = I2 × V2V1 = 1.2 × 11.5 ÷ 230 = 0.060 A.
nye.17: Now let’s check it with power conservation: P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W and P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W — equal, exactly like an ideal transformer demands! Notice we used RMS values the whole way through: transformer ratios work fine with RMS, peak or peak-to-peak values, as long as you stick with one type consistently on both sides.
nye.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 is energy conservation showing up yet again. The volt is a joule per coulomb — that’s W = qV from AOS 2 — so P = VI is counting joules delivered every second, and an ideal transformer has got to pass every single joule on through!
nye.19: A transmission system has three parts wired up in series: a supply, a pair of long lines, and a load — which might be a single machine, a house, or a whole town! The two lines get modelled as one single resistor of total resistance Rline, as shown in Figure 8.2. With no transformers in the system, the supply, the lines and the load make one series loop, so a single current Iline flows through every bit of it.
nye.20: The lines obey Ohm’s law just like any other resistor: pushing the current through them costs you a potential difference, and burns off power as heat.
nye.21: Both formulas use the line current and the line resistance. The voltage that shows up in them is Vdrop, the drop along the lines themselves — never the supply voltage! The supply voltage splits between the lines and the load, so the load only gets what’s left over: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.
nye.22: Trap The single most damaging mistake in this whole topic is jamming the supply voltage where Vdrop belongs — like computing the line current as Vsupply ÷ Rline, or the loss as Vsupply² ÷ Rline. The full supply voltage is never across the lines alone; only Vdrop = IlineRline is! Work from the line current, or from the difference between the voltages at the two ends of the lines — never from the supply voltage directly.
nye.23: Shanna’s community energy group runs a small wind turbine that generates 20 kW, and that power feeds a dairy 2.0 km away through lines of total resistance 4.0 Ω. All voltages and currents are RMS values, and the turbine pumps out its full 20 kW in both setups below. Let’s compare them!
nye.24: Without transformers, the power leaves the turbine at 500 V. We find the line current from P = VI: Iline = 20 000 ÷ 500 = 40 A. The lines burn off Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — a whopping 32% of the generated power! The drop along the lines is Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V, so the dairy only gets 500 − 160 = 340 V and 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW.
nye.25: With transformers, now watch what happens — a step-up transformer at the turbine cranks the transmission voltage up tenfold, to 5.0 kV, and a step-down transformer at the dairy brings it back to a usable level. That same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The loss collapses to Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — just 0.32%! — and the drop shrinks to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and a full 19.9 kW makes it to the dairy.
nye.26: Those numbers lay the general rule right out in the open. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, so transmitting at n times the voltage slashes the current by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss tumbles by a factor of n²! Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A and the loss fell a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. That right there is the entire case for high-voltage transmission — the only alternative, lowering Rline with thicker wire, means stringing far heavier and far more expensive conductors on every single tower.
nye.27: And stepping down again at the far end is every bit as essential: it brings the voltage back to the safe, standard 230 V that your appliances are built for — and it cranks the current back up, which means the biggest currents in the whole system flow in the local wiring, not in the transmission lines!
nye.28: Key idea For a fixed power delivery, stepping the transmission voltage up by a factor of n cuts the line current by a factor of n and the line loss by a factor of n². Losses depend on the line current and the line resistance — never compute them from the supply voltage.

einstein.3: The Australian mains supply is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. Take a plain time-average and you get nothing — every positive half-cycle is undone by the negative half-cycle that follows. And yet the supply still heats. Heating cares nothing for which way the current runs, so power arrives on both halves of the swing. To describe an AC supply, then, we cannot lean on a simple average; we must build a number out of power itself.
levin.2: The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is the constant DC voltage that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. The RMS current is defined the same way. Notice that the definition says nothing about sine waves at all; but for the sinusoid an alternator gives, the RMS values come out to be the peak values divided by √2:
levin.3: The Australian mains is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. For the purpose of power, every outlet behaves as a 230 V DC supply — yet the true voltage swings between +325 V and −325 V (since 230 × √2 = 325 V), a peak-to-peak reach of about 650 V, and it completes fifty of these swings each second, one cycle every 1/50 = 0.020 s.
levin.4: Three voltages describe the one sinusoid: Vpeak = √2 × VRMS, the peak-to-peak value Vp–p = 2Vpeak, and so Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. The RMS value is always the smallest of the three. And a stated AC voltage — on a power point, on an appliance plate — is an RMS value, unless it tells you otherwise. The same three descriptions, with the same conversions, hold for the current.
levin.5: Because the RMS values are built from power equivalence, the DC power relation carries straight across to AC:
levin.6: Key idea An RMS value is the constant DC voltage (or current) that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
levin.7: Trap A globe burns just as bright on a 6.0 V battery as on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What they share is the average power — not the average voltage, which for the AC supply is zero, and not the maximum voltage, which for the AC supply is 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V. levin.8: A transformer is two coils wound apart on a closed iron core — nothing moves, and nothing electrical joins the one coil to the other. Alternating current in the primary makes it an electromagnet, its magnetic flux rising and falling with the supply, and the iron core gathers almost all of that flux and walks it around the loop and through the secondary. The very same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. Watch the loss collapse: Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — only 0.32% — and the drop shrinks to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and a full 19.9 kW reaches the dairy.
veritasium.26: Those numbers aren't a fluke — they're the general rule in action. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, so transmitting at n times the voltage cuts the current by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss drops by a factor of n². Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A and the loss fell a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. That, in one line, is the entire case for high-voltage transmission. The only alternative — cutting Rline with thicker wire — means hanging far heavier and far more expensive conductors on every single tower.
veritasium.27: Stepping back down at the destination matters just as much: it returns the voltage to the safe, standard 230 V that appliances are built to run on — and in doing so it raises the current right back up, which means the biggest currents anywhere in the system flow through your local wiring, not through the transmission lines.
veritasium.28: Key idea For a fixed power delivery, stepping the transmission voltage up by a factor of n cuts the line current by a factor of n and the line loss by a factor of n². Losses depend on the line current and the line resistance — never compute them from the supply voltage.

attenborough.0: Electricity is born far from the places where it is needed — out beyond the towns, out where the turbines turn. To carry it that distance, we must first learn its language. On this page we shall build that language for an AC supply — rms, peak and peak-to-peak — then meet the transformer, that quiet device which trades voltage for current, and arrive, at last, at the losses along the lines: the very losses that make high-voltage transmission the only economical way to move power across a continent.
attenborough.1: The coil of an alternator turns at a steady, untiring rate, and so its output voltage rises and falls in a smooth sinusoid, swinging between +Vpeak and −Vpeak many times in a single second. Take a plain average of that voltage over time and you find nothing at all — zero — for every positive half-cycle is undone by the negative half-cycle that follows close behind. And yet the supply still warms a wire, still delivers heating power. The secret is this: heating cares nothing for the direction of the current, so power flows on both half-cycles alike. To describe such a supply, then, we need a value founded not on a simple average, but on power itself.
attenborough.2: The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is the constant DC voltage that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component — no more, no less. The RMS current is defined in just the same way. Notice that the definition says nothing whatever about sine waves; yet for the sinusoidal output of an alternator, the RMS values turn out, beautifully, to be the peak values divided by √2:
attenborough.3: The Australian mains supply is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. For the purposes of power, every outlet behaves as though it were a 230 V DC supply — and yet the true voltage is restless, swinging all the while between +325 V and −325 V (for 230 × √2 = 325 V), a peak-to-peak journey of some 650 V, completing fifty full cycles every second, one cycle in 1/50 = 0.020 s.
attenborough.4: Three voltages describe one and the same sinusoid: Vpeak = √2 × VRMS; the peak-to-peak value Vp–p = 2Vpeak; and therefore Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. The RMS value is always the smallest of the three, the most modest. And a stated AC voltage — printed on a power point, or stamped upon an appliance plate — is an RMS value, unless it tells you otherwise. The very same three descriptions, with the very same conversions, hold for the current too.
attenborough.5: And because RMS values are built, from the very beginning, upon power equivalence, the familiar DC power relation carries across to AC untouched:
attenborough.6: Key idea An RMS value is the constant DC voltage (or current) that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
attenborough.7: Trap A globe shines just as brightly on a 6.0 V battery as on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What they share is the average power — not the average voltage, which is zero for the AC supply, and not the maximum voltage, which for the AC supply reaches 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V. To read the mains label “230 V” as a peak value is to make the very same mistake, only turned about.
attenborough.8: A transformer is two separate coils, wound upon a closed iron core — no moving parts, and no electrical connection whatever between the coils. Alternating current in the primary coil turns it into an electromagnet, whose magnetic flux alternates in step with the supply, and the iron core gathers up almost all of that flux and guides it faithfully around the loop and through the secondary coil. The flux threading every turn of the secondary is therefore changing, ceaselessly, and so an EMF is induced across it — electromagnetic induction, with nothing rotating at all, as shown in Figure 8.1.
attenborough.9: Every turn of the secondary encircles the very same changing flux, and so each turn adds its equal share to the output: double the secondary turns, and you double the output voltage. For an ideal transformer — one that loses no power at all — the voltages stand in the ratio of the turns:
attenborough.10: A step-up transformer carries more secondary turns than primary turns (N2 > N1), and delivers a higher voltage than it was fed; a step-down transformer does the reverse. Transformers take their names from what they do to the voltage — and it is the ratio of the turns, not their actual number, that decides everything. The output alternates at the very same frequency as the input: a transformer rescales the sinusoid; it never reshapes it.
attenborough.11: An ideal transformer passes every last watt of its input power across to the output:
attenborough.12: Trap A step-up transformer is no amplifier. Raise the voltage by a factor of n, and the current falls by the very same factor — for the output power can never, ever exceed the input power. The current ratio in the transformer equation sits upside-down beside the other two: the high-voltage side of a transformer always carries the smaller current.
attenborough.13: The whole mechanism rests upon one thing alone — changing flux — and here is why a transformer must have AC. Connect the primary to a battery instead, and the steady current makes a flux that holds constant in the core; the flux through the secondary no longer changes, so no EMF is induced, and the output falls to nothing. Only in the fleeting instant the battery is connected, or disconnected — the only moments the flux stirs at all — does a brief pulse appear, and vanish. A grid that leans upon transformers must therefore be an AC grid.
attenborough.14: Ishva’s phone charger steps the 230 V RMS Australian mains supply down to 11.5 V RMS. The transformer within is modelled as ideal, with 600 primary turns, and the charger supplies 1.2 A RMS from its secondary coil.
attenborough.16: Current drawn from the mains: I1 = I2 × V2V1 = 1.2 × 11.5 ÷ 230 = 0.060 A.
attenborough.17: And now let us check it by the conservation of power: P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W, while P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W — equal, exactly as an ideal transformer demands. RMS values were used throughout; and yet the transformer ratios will work as happily with RMS, peak or peak-to-peak values, provided one single type is used consistently on both sides.
attenborough.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 is, once again, the conservation of energy at work. The volt is a joule per coulomb — that is W = qV from AOS 2 — so P = VI counts the joules delivered each second, and an ideal transformer must hand every joule on, keeping none for itself.
attenborough.19: A transmission system has three parts, joined in series: a supply, a pair of long lines, and a load — a single machine, a house, or an entire town. The two lines are modelled as one resistor, of total resistance Rline, as shown in Figure 8.2. With no transformers anywhere in the system, the supply, the lines and the load form a single series loop, and so one current — Iline — flows through them all.
attenborough.20: The lines obey Ohm’s law, just as any other resistor must: driving the current through them costs a potential difference, and bleeds away power as heat.
attenborough.21: Both formulas call upon the line current and the line resistance. The voltage that appears within them is Vdrop, the drop along the lines themselves — never, ever the supply voltage. For the supply voltage divides itself between the lines and the load, and the load receives only what remains: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.
attenborough.22: Trap The most ruinous error in all this topic is to set the supply voltage where Vdrop belongs — computing, say, the line current as Vsupply ÷ Rline, or the loss as Vsupply² ÷ Rline. The full supply voltage is never across the lines alone; only Vdrop = IlineRline is. Work always from the line current, or from the difference between the voltages at the two ends of the lines — never from the supply voltage directly.
attenborough.23: Shanna’s community energy group runs a small wind turbine that generates 20 kW, fed to a dairy 2.0 km away through lines of total resistance 4.0 Ω. All voltages and currents are RMS values, and the turbine supplies its full 20 kW in both arrangements below.
attenborough.24: Without transformers, the power leaves the turbine at 500 V. The line current follows from P = VI: Iline = 20 000 ÷ 500 = 40 A. The lines dissipate Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — fully 32% of the generated power, gone to warm the air. The drop along the lines is Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V, so the dairy receives only 500 − 160 = 340 V and 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW.
attenborough.25: With transformers, a step-up transformer at the turbine lifts the transmission voltage tenfold, to 5.0 kV, and a step-down transformer at the dairy returns it to a usable level. The same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. And watch the loss collapse: Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — a mere 0.32% — and the drop falls with it to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and 19.9 kW reaches the dairy at last.
attenborough.26: The numbers lay bare the general rule. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, so transmitting at n times the voltage cuts the current by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss tumbles by a factor of n². Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A, and the loss fell a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. This, and nothing more, is the entire case for high-voltage transmission — for the only alternative, lowering Rline with thicker wire, would mean hanging far heavier, far costlier conductors upon every tower across the land.
attenborough.27: And stepping down again at the destination is every bit as necessary: it returns the voltage to the safe, standard 230 V that appliances are built for — and it raises the current once more, so that the greatest currents in the whole system flow not out along the lonely transmission lines, but here, close to home, in the local wiring.
attenborough.28: Key idea For a fixed power delivery, stepping the transmission voltage up by a factor of n cuts the line current by a factor of n and the line loss by a factor of n². Losses depend on the line current and the line resistance — never compute them from the supply voltage.

greekepic.0: Sing, Muse, of the lightning forged in places far from the hearths that hunger for it: for electricity is born a long way from where it is spent. This song first builds the language for naming an AC supply — rms, peak and peak-to-peak — then summons the transformer, that wise exchanger of voltage for current, and ends at the losses in the lines, the toll that makes high-voltage transmission the one road by which power may be carried without ruin.
greekepic.1: The coil of an alternator turns at a steady, tireless rate, and so its output voltage rises and falls in the manner of the sine, swinging between +Vpeak and −Vpeak many times in a single breath of the second. Reckon the plain time-average of this voltage and you find nothing — zero — for every rising half-cycle is undone by the falling half-cycle that comes hard upon it; and yet the AC supply pours forth heating power still. Heat heeds not the direction of the current, and so the supply delivers its power upon both halves of the cycle alike. To name an AC supply truly, therefore, one must forge a value built upon power, and not upon a mere average.
greekepic.2: The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is decreed to be that constant DC voltage which would raise the same average power in the same resistive component. The RMS current is named in the selfsame way. This decree speaks no word of sine waves; yet for the sinusoidal output of an alternator, the RMS values are revealed to be the peak values divided by √2:
greekepic.3: The mains supply of the Australian land is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. For the purposes of power, every outlet bears itself as a 230 V DC supply would; but the true voltage swings between +325 V and −325 V (for 230 × √2 = 325 V) — a peak-to-peak swing of some 650 V — and completes fifty cycles in each passing second, one whole cycle every 1/50 = 0.020 s.
greekepic.4: Three voltages name the one and the same sinusoid: Vpeak = √2 × VRMS; the peak-to-peak value Vp–p = 2Vpeak; and therefore Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. The RMS value is ever the least of the three, and a stated AC voltage — graven upon a power point or upon an appliance plate — is an RMS value, save where it proclaims otherwise. These same three namings, with the same conversions, hold true for the current as well.
greekepic.5: Because the RMS values are forged from the equivalence of power, the DC relation of power passes over directly into the realm of AC:
greekepic.6: Key idea An RMS value is that constant DC voltage (or current) which would raise the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
greekepic.7: Trap A globe burns with equal brightness upon a 6.0 V battery and upon a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What stands the same is the average power — not the average voltage, which is zero for the AC supply, nor yet the greatest voltage, which is 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V for the AC supply. To read the mains label “230 V” as a peak value is to commit the selfsame error, only turned about.
greekepic.8: A transformer is two coils set apart, wound upon a closed core of iron, with no moving part and no metal road joining the one coil to the other. Alternating current in the primary coil makes of it an electromagnet whose magnetic flux alternates in step with the supply, and the iron core shepherds well-nigh all of that flux around the loop and through the secondary coil. The flux threading every turn of the secondary is therefore forever changing, and so an EMF is summoned across it — electromagnetic induction with nothing whatsoever set spinning, as is shown in Figure 8.1.
greekepic.9: Every turn of the secondary girds the same changing flux, and so each turn lends an equal share to the output: double the turns of the secondary, and you double the voltage that comes forth. For an ideal transformer — one that loses no power at all — the voltages stand in the ratio of the turns:
greekepic.10: A step-up transformer bears more turns upon its secondary than upon its primary (N2 > N1) and gives forth a higher voltage than it is fed; a step-down transformer is its mirror and its reverse. Transformers take their names from what they work upon the voltage, and it is the ratio of the turns, not the bare count of them, that holds dominion. The output alternates at the same frequency as the input — for a transformer rescales the sinusoid; it does not reshape it.
greekepic.11: An ideal transformer hands over all of its input power to the output:
greekepic.12: Trap A step-up transformer is no amplifier — let none be deceived. To raise the voltage by a factor of n is to lower the current by that same factor, for the output power can never overreach the input power. The current ratio in the transformer equation stands turned upon its head against the other two: the high-voltage side of a transformer ever carries the lesser current.
greekepic.13: The whole working rests upon changing flux, and herein lies the reason transformers must have AC. Should the primary be bound instead to a battery, the steady current raises a constant flux within the core; the flux through the secondary changes no longer, and so no EMF is summoned and the output is nothing. Only at the very instant the battery is joined or severed — the sole moments when the flux changes — does a brief pulse spring forth. A grid that leans upon transformers must therefore be, of necessity, an AC grid.

greekepic.17: Prove it by the conservation of power: P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W and P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W — equal, as an ideal transformer demands. RMS values were borne throughout: transformer ratios keep faith with RMS, peak or peak-to-peak values alike, so long as one kind is held to steadfastly on both sides.
greekepic.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 calls once more upon the conservation of energy. The volt is a joule for each coulomb — that is W = qV from AOS 2 — and so P = VI tallies the joules delivered in each second, and an ideal transformer must pass on every joule it receives.

The supply voltage parts itself between the lines and the load, and so the load receives but the remainder: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.

The whole supply voltage is never across the lines alone; only Vdrop = IlineRline is. The lines pour out Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — 32% of the power generated. The drop along the lines is Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V, and so the dairy receives but 500 − 160 = 340 V and 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW.
greekepic.25: With transformers, a step-up transformer at the turbine raises the transmission voltage tenfold, to 5.0 kV, and a step-down transformer at the dairy returns it to a level fit for use. The same 20 kW now journeys at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The loss collapses to Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — a mere 0.32% — and the drop to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so that the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and 19.9 kW arrives at the dairy.
greekepic.26: The numbers lay bare the general decree. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, and so to transmit at n times the voltage is to cut the current by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss falls by a factor of n². Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A and the loss fell a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. cnpop.0: 电是在离你十万八千里的地方发出来的，跟用它的地方根本不挨着。这一页我们先把描述交流电的那套黑话练熟——有效值、峰值、峰峰值——再请出变压器这位拿电压换电流的狠角色，最后掰扯清楚线路损耗：正是它，让高压输电成了搬运电能唯一划算的活法。
cnpop.1: 交流发电机里的线圈匀速地转啊转，于是输出电压就像荡秋千一样按正弦规律来回甩，一秒钟内在 +Vpeak 和 −Vpeak 之间反复横跳无数次。你要是老老实实对时间取个平均，结果是零——每个正半周都被紧跟着的负半周一笔勾销，干干净净——可交流电分明还能烧水点灯啊。原因在于：发热这事根本不挑电流方向，正半周负半周都在乖乖输出功率。所以想把交流电说明白，就得搬出一个建立在功率上的数值，而不是那个白搭的简单平均。
cnpop.2: 交流电压的方均根值（RMS 有效值），定义就是这么个东西：一个恒定的直流电压，要是它能在同一个电阻元件上掏出同样的平均功率，那它的大小就是有效值。有效电流也照这个套路定义。注意，这定义里压根没提正弦波三个字；不过对于发电机那种正弦输出，有效值算出来正好就是峰值除以 √2：
cnpop.3: 澳大利亚市电是 230 V RMS、50 Hz。论起功率来，每个插座都跟一个 230 V 的直流电源没两样，可实际电压一直在 +325 V 和 −325 V 之间狂飙（因为 230 × √2 = 325 V）——峰峰那么一甩差不多 650 V——而且一秒钟跑满五十个来回，每 1/50 = 0.020 s 走完一整圈。
cnpop.4: 同一条正弦曲线，能用三个电压来形容它：Vpeak = √2 × VRMS，峰峰值 Vp–p = 2Vpeak，于是顺理成章 Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS。三个里头有效值永远是最小的那位，而插座上、电器铭牌上写的交流电压，没特别说明的话就一律是有效值。电流也是同样这三套说法、同样的换算，一模一样照搬。
cnpop.5: 正因为有效值是从功率等效里抠出来的，那套直流的功率公式就能原封不动地搬到交流上来用：
cnpop.6: Key idea 有效值就是那个恒定直流电压（或电流）——它能在同一个电阻元件上掏出同样的平均功率。对正弦交流而言，有效值 = 峰值 ÷ √2。
cnpop.7: Trap 一只灯泡接在 6.0 V 电池上，和接在 6.0 V RMS 的交流电源上，亮度一模一样。一样的是平均功率——可不是平均电压，交流那边平均电压是零；也不是最大电压，交流那边最大电压是 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V。把市电铭牌上那个“230 V”当成峰值来读，就是同一个坑反着踩了一遍。
cnpop.8: 变压器其实就是两组分开的线圈，绕在一个闭合的铁芯上，没有任何转动的部件，两组线圈之间也压根不通电。初级线圈里灌进交流电，它就摇身一变成了电磁铁，磁通量跟着电源一起来回变；而那个铁芯像个引路人，把几乎全部磁通量沿着回路领着走，穿过次级线圈。这么一来，穿过次级每一匝的磁通量都在不停地变，于是次级两端就被感应出一个电动势——整个过程啥都没转，纯靠电磁感应，正如 Figure 8.1 所示。
cnpop.9: 次级的每一匝都把同一份变化的磁通量圈在里头，所以每匝对输出的贡献都是平起平坐：次级匝数翻一倍，输出电压就翻一倍。对一个理想变压器来说——也就是一点功率都不糟蹋的那种——电压之比就等于匝数之比：
cnpop.10: 升压变压器的次级匝数比初级多（N2 > N1），喂进去多少电压它就吐出更高的电压；降压变压器则反过来。变压器的名字看的是它把电压怎么折腾，而且真正说了算的是匝数之比，不是匝数本身那个具体数字。输出的振荡频率跟输入一模一样——变压器只是把正弦波重新缩放一下个头，可没把它的形状改了。
cnpop.11: 理想变压器会把输入功率原样全部送到输出端，一点不留：
cnpop.12: Trap 升压变压器可不是放大器。把电压拉高 n 倍，电流就老老实实降到原来的 n 分之一，因为输出功率永远翻不过输入功率这座山。变压器方程里电流那个比是倒着写的，跟另外两个反着来：变压器上电压高的那一侧，扛的永远是更小的电流。
cnpop.13: 整套机制的命根子就是变化的磁通量，这也正是变压器非交流不可的原因。要是把初级接到电池上，那股稳稳的直流电在铁芯里只会撑起一个恒定不变的磁通量；穿过次级的磁通量不变了，自然就没电动势被感应出来，输出直接挂零。只有在电池接通或断开的那一刹那——磁通量唯一会动的两个瞬间——才会蹦出一个短促的脉冲。所以一张靠变压器吃饭的电网，注定只能是交流电网。
cnpop.14: Ishva 的手机充电器把 230 V RMS 的澳大利亚市电降压成 11.5 V RMS。里头的变压器按理想来建模，初级 600 匝，充电器从次级线圈输出 1.2 A RMS。
cnpop.16: 从市电那头抽出来的电流：I1 = I2 × V2V1 = 1.2 × 11.5 ÷ 230 = 0.060 A。
cnpop.17: 拿功率守恒来验一下：P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W，P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W——分毫不差地相等，理想变压器就该这样。从头到尾用的都是有效值：变压器那套比例，有效值、峰值还是峰峰值都能套，只要两边自始至终用的是同一种就行。
cnpop.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 又一次把能量守恒搬了出来。伏特就是每库仑多少焦耳——这正是 W = qV from AOS 2——所以 P = VI 数的是每秒送出去多少焦耳，而理想变压器必须把每一颗焦耳原封不动地往下传。
cnpop.19: 一套输电系统串成一条线，分三段：一个电源、一对长长的线路，还有一个负载——可以是一台机器、一户人家，也可以是整座城镇。那两根线被建模成一个总电阻为 Rline 的电阻，正如 Figure 8.2 所示。系统里要是没装变压器，电源、线路和负载就围成一个串联回路，于是一股电流 Iline 一路穿过它们仨。
cnpop.20: 线路跟别的电阻一样，照样得听欧姆定律的话：硬把电流推过去，是要赔上一段电势差的，还得把功率当成热白白散掉。
cnpop.21: 两个公式用的都是线路电流和线路电阻。里头露脸的那个电压是 Vdrop，也就是线路自己那一段上的压降——绝不是电源电压。电源电压会在线路和负载之间分家，所以负载只能拿到剩下的零头：Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop，还有 Pload = Psupply − Ploss。
cnpop.22: Trap 这一节里最致命的翻车，就是把电源电压硬塞到 Vdrop 该待的位置上——比如把线路电流算成 Vsupply ÷ Rline，或者把损耗算成 Vsupply² ÷ Rline。整个电源电压从来不会单独压在线路上；压在线上的只有 Vdrop = IlineRline 这么一点。要么从线路电流下手，要么从线路两端那两个电压的差值下手——千万别拿电源电压直接开干。
cnpop.23: Shanna 的社区能源小组捣鼓了一台小风力发电机，发出 20 kW，通过总电阻 4.0 Ω 的线路送到 2.0 km 外的一家奶牛场。所有电压和电流都是有效值，而且下面这两种方案里风机都把它那满满的 20 kW 全数供上。
cnpop.24: 不装变压器时，电以 500 V 离开风机。线路电流由 P = VI 算出：Iline = 20 000 ÷ 500 = 40 A。线路散掉的功率 Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW——足足占了发出功率的 32%。线路上的压降 Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V，所以奶牛场只剩 500 − 160 = 340 V 和 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW 拿到手。
cnpop.25: 装上变压器后，风机这头的升压变压器把输电电压一口气拉高十倍，到 5.0 kV，奶牛场那头的降压变压器再把它打回能用的水平。同样这 20 kW，现在以 Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A 上路。损耗当场塌缩成 Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W——才区区 0.32%——压降也降到 Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V，于是降压变压器收到 5000 − 16 = 4984 V，最终有 19.9 kW 抵达奶牛场。
cnpop.26: 这几个数字活脱脱把那条普适规律摆在了台面上。灌进线路的功率是定死的，又有 P = VI，所以用 n 倍的电压来输电，电流就砍到原来的 n 分之一；又因为 Ploss = Iline²Rline，损耗就暴跌到原来的 n² 分之一。这里 n = 10：电流从 40 A 掉到 4.0 A，损耗整整缩了一百倍，从 6.4 kW 跌到 64 W。这就是高压输电的全部底气——另一条路，靠加粗导线把 Rline 压下去，意味着每座铁塔上都得吊起重得多、也贵得多的导线。
cnpop.27: 到了目的地再降压，同样是非干不可的事：它把电压送回安全、标准的 230 V，那才是各种电器量身打造去配的；而它又把电流抬了回来，所以全系统最大的那些电流，是在家门口的本地线路里流，而不是在输电干线上跑。
cnpop.28: Key idea 在送电功率定死的前提下，把输电电压抬高 n 倍，线路电流就砍到 n 分之一，线路损耗砍到 n² 分之一。损耗只看线路电流和线路电阻——千万别拿电源电压去算它。

ai.0: The power lighting your room was almost certainly made hundreds of kilometres from where you’re sitting. This page builds the words you need to talk about an AC supply — rms, peak and peak-to-peak — then meets the transformer, the device that trades voltage for current, and finishes with the line losses that make high-voltage transmission the only sensible way to shift power across a country.
ai.1: The coil of an alternator spins at a steady rate, so the voltage it pumps out rises and falls as a smooth sine wave, swinging between +Vpeak and −Vpeak many times every second. Try to take a plain time-average of that voltage and you get zero — every positive half-cycle is wiped out by the negative half-cycle right behind it. And yet an AC supply still heats your toaster. The trick is that heating doesn’t care which way the current flows, so power gets delivered on both half-cycles. To describe an AC supply honestly, then, you need a number built on power, not on a naive average.
ai.2: The root-mean-square (RMS) value of an alternating voltage is defined as the steady DC voltage that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. RMS current is defined the very same way. Notice the definition never once mentions sine waves — but for the sinusoidal output of an alternator, the RMS values turn out to be the peak values divided by √2:
ai.3: The Australian mains supply is 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. As far as power is concerned, every outlet behaves exactly like a 230 V DC supply — yet the actual voltage is swinging between +325 V and −325 V (since 230 × √2 = 325 V), a peak-to-peak swing of roughly 650 V, racing through fifty full cycles every second, one cycle every 1/50 = 0.020 s.
ai.4: Three voltages describe one and the same sinusoid: the peak, Vpeak = √2 × VRMS; the peak-to-peak value, Vp–p = 2Vpeak; and putting those together, Vp–p = 2√2 × VRMS. The RMS value is always the smallest of the three, and whenever you read an AC voltage — stamped on a power point or printed on an appliance plate — it’s an RMS value unless told otherwise. The exact same three descriptions, with the exact same conversions, apply to the current as well.
ai.5: Because RMS values are forged from power equivalence in the first place, the DC power relation carries straight over to AC, untouched:
ai.6: Key idea An RMS value is the steady DC voltage (or current) that would develop the same average power in the same resistive component. For sinusoidal AC, RMS = peak ÷ √2.
ai.7: Trap A globe glows just as brightly on a 6.0 V battery as on a 6.0 V RMS AC supply. What matches is the average power — not the average voltage, which is zero for the AC supply, and not the maximum voltage, which for the AC supply reaches 6.0 × √2 = 8.5 V. Reading the mains label “230 V” as if it were a peak value is exactly this same blunder, just running the other way.
ai.8: A transformer is nothing more than two separate coils wound on a closed iron core — no moving parts, and no electrical connection between the coils at all. Alternating current in the primary coil turns it into an electromagnet whose magnetic flux alternates in step with the supply, and the iron core shepherds nearly all of that flux around the loop and on through the secondary coil. So the flux threading every turn of the secondary is forever changing, and an EMF is induced across it — electromagnetic induction with nothing rotating at all, as shown in Figure 8.1.
ai.9: Every turn of the secondary wraps around the same changing flux, so each turn pitches in equally to the output: double the secondary turns and you double the output voltage. For an ideal transformer — one that wastes no power — the voltages sit in the ratio of the turns:
ai.10: A step-up transformer has more secondary turns than primary turns (N2 > N1) and hands back a higher voltage than it was fed; a step-down transformer does the opposite. Transformers are named for what they do to the voltage, and what counts is the ratio of the turns, never their raw number. The output alternates at the same frequency as the input — a transformer rescales the sinusoid, it doesn’t reshape it.
ai.11: An ideal transformer passes every bit of its input power straight through to the output:
ai.12: Trap A step-up transformer is not an amplifier — don’t be fooled. Push the voltage up by a factor of n and the current drops by that very same factor, because the output power can never beat the input power. The current ratio in the transformer equation is flipped relative to the other two: the high-voltage side of a transformer always carries the smaller current.
ai.13: The whole mechanism leans on changing flux, and that is precisely why transformers demand AC. Hook the primary up to a battery instead, and the steady current sets up a constant flux in the core; the flux through the secondary stops changing, so no EMF is induced and the output is dead zero. Only at the instant you connect or disconnect the battery — the only moments the flux actually changes — does a brief pulse flicker out. A grid that lives on transformers must therefore be an AC grid.
ai.14: Ishva’s phone charger steps the 230 V RMS Australian mains supply down to 11.5 V RMS. Model the transformer inside as ideal, with 600 primary turns, and the charger delivers 1.2 A RMS from its secondary coil.
ai.16: Current drawn from the mains: I1 = I2 × V2V1 = 1.2 × 11.5 ÷ 230 = 0.060 A.
ai.17: Check it with power conservation: P2 = 11.5 × 1.2 = 13.8 W and P1 = 230 × 0.060 = 13.8 W — equal, exactly as an ideal transformer demands. RMS values were used the whole way through: transformer ratios are happy with RMS, peak or peak-to-peak values, just as long as you stick to one type consistently on both sides.
ai.18: Connects V1I1 = V2I2 is energy conservation showing up yet again. The volt is a joule per coulomb — that’s W = qV from AOS 2 — so P = VI is really counting joules delivered every second, and an ideal transformer has no choice but to pass every one of those joules on.
ai.19: A transmission system comes in three parts wired in series: a supply, a pair of long lines, and a load — maybe a single machine, maybe a house, maybe a whole town. We model the two lines as one resistor of total resistance Rline, as shown in Figure 8.2. With no transformers anywhere in the system, the supply, the lines and the load form one series loop, so a single current Iline flows through every part of it.
ai.20: The lines obey Ohm’s law just like any other resistor: forcing the current through them costs a potential difference, and bleeds power away as heat.
ai.21: Both formulas run on the line current and the line resistance. The voltage that belongs in them is Vdrop, the drop along the lines themselves — never the supply voltage. The supply voltage splits between the lines and the load, so the load only ever gets what’s left over: Vload = Vsupply − Vdrop and Pload = Psupply − Ploss.
ai.22: Trap The single most damaging mistake in this whole topic is dropping the supply voltage into the slot where Vdrop belongs — say, working out the line current as Vsupply ÷ Rline, or the loss as Vsupply² ÷ Rline. The full supply voltage is never across the lines alone; only Vdrop = IlineRline is. Always work from the line current, or from the difference between the voltages at the two ends of the lines — never from the supply voltage directly.
ai.23: Shanna’s community energy group runs a small wind turbine that generates 20 kW, piped to a dairy 2.0 km away through lines of total resistance 4.0 Ω. Treat all voltages and currents as RMS values, and take it that the turbine supplies its full 20 kW in both arrangements below.
ai.24: Without transformers, the power leaves the turbine at 500 V. Find the line current from P = VI: Iline = 20 000 ÷ 500 = 40 A. The lines dissipate Ploss = Iline²Rline = 40² × 4.0 = 6.4 × 10³ W = 6.4 kW — a brutal 32% of everything generated. The drop along the lines is Vdrop = 40 × 4.0 = 160 V, so the dairy is left with only 500 − 160 = 340 V and 20 − 6.4 = 13.6 kW.
ai.25: With transformers, a step-up transformer at the turbine lifts the transmission voltage tenfold, up to 5.0 kV, and a step-down transformer at the dairy brings it back to something usable. That same 20 kW now travels at Iline = 20 000 ÷ 5000 = 4.0 A. The loss collapses to Ploss = 4.0² × 4.0 = 64 W — a mere 0.32% — and the drop to Vdrop = 4.0 × 4.0 = 16 V, so the step-down transformer receives 5000 − 16 = 4984 V and a healthy 19.9 kW reaches the dairy.
ai.26: Those numbers spell out the general rule. The power fed into the lines is fixed, and P = VI, so transmitting at n times the voltage slashes the current by a factor of n; and since Ploss = Iline²Rline, the loss tumbles by a factor of n². Here n = 10: the current fell from 40 A to 4.0 A and the loss dropped a hundredfold, from 6.4 kW to 64 W. That, in a nutshell, is the entire case for high-voltage transmission — the only alternative, dropping Rline by using thicker wire, means stringing far heavier and far more expensive conductors from every single tower.
ai.27: Stepping back down at the destination matters just as much: it brings the voltage home to the safe, standard 230 V that appliances are built to run on — and it pushes the current back up, which is why the biggest currents in the whole system flow in the local wiring, not out in the transmission lines.
ai.28: Key idea For a fixed power delivery, stepping the transmission voltage up by a factor of n cuts the line current by a factor of n and the line loss by a factor of n². Losses ride on the line current and the line resistance — never compute them from the supply voltage.
